g.data-narration: Welcome to Binary Search Trees! Today we'll explore this fundamental data structure used in databases, file systems, and more.
g.text: A Complete Visual Guide | university 2022 Scheme - CSE

g.data-narration: A Binary Search Tree is a hierarchical data structure where each node has at most two children with a specific ordering property.
g.text: What is a Binary Search Tree?
A BST is a node-based data structure where:
• Each node has at most two children (left and right)
• Left subtree values < Parent value < Right subtree values

g.data-narration: Binary Search Trees power many real-world applications including database indexing for fast lookups and file system navigation.
g.text: Efficient operations with average O(log n):

g.data-narration: Here's a visual example of a BST. Notice how all values in the left subtree are less than 8, and all values in the right subtree are greater.
A BST must satisfy three key properties: unique values, left subtree smaller, and right subtree larger than the node.
g.text: Key Properties of Binary Search Tree
Each node has a unique key

g.data-narration: To search in a BST, start at root and compare: go left if target is smaller, go right if larger, until found or null.
g.text: Algorithm: Start at root, compare target value

g.data-narration: Insert follows the search path: traverse left or right until finding an empty spot, then place the new node there.
g.text: Insert Operation: Adding a New Node
Steps: Navigate tree like search, insert at first empty position

g.data-narration: Delete has three cases: leaf node (simple removal), one child (replace with child), or two children (use in-order successor).
There are three types of BSTs: full (every node has 2 children), empty (no nodes), and complete (all levels filled left-to-right).
BSTs offer efficient O(log n) average operations, maintain sorted order automatically, and can implement other data structures like heaps.
g.text: ✓ Can implement heaps and priority queues

g.data-narration: Let's compare BST complexity with other data structures. Hash tables offer O(1) average operations but lose ordering.
Here's the essential C code for BST operations: node structure, createNode, insertNode, searchNode, and deleteNode functions.
g.text: struct Node { int data; struct Node* left; struct Node* right; };

g.data-narration: In summary: BSTs provide efficient search, insert, and delete operations with O(log n) average time. They maintain sorted order but can become unbalanced.
g.text: BSTs: Efficient O(log n) operations | Maintains sorted order | Can degrade to O(n) if unbalanced
